cases.title: Regional feature flags during control-plane isolation

public.objective: Keep critical services operable during a feature-flag control-plane outage without allowing stale regional state to produce an unsafe split rollout.
facts: Four regions cache flag values for fifteen minutes and then fail closed for flags marked safety-critical.
The flag service writes through one home region and replicates asynchronously with delays up to ninety seconds.
Two payment flags must change atomically across all regions, while most presentation flags may diverge temporarily.
Services currently cannot distinguish a deliberately pinned value from a cache value that merely expired.
A regional network isolation last quarter caused one region to roll back while three regions continued the new path.
public.decision_request: Choose the consistency classes, isolation behavior, rollout cohort, and evidence required before expanding the new flag contract.
plausible_paths: Require globally synchronous reads for every flag.
Introduce explicit flag classes with pinned regional fallbacks and atomic epochs for coupled safety flags.
Keep the current cache behavior and shorten time to live for every flag.
material_constraints: Coupled payment flags cannot safely take different epochs.
The home region can be unreachable while local service traffic remains healthy.
Most flags do not justify a global request-path dependency.
Operators need to distinguish intentional pinning from stale cache state.
Rollback must not create another mixed epoch.
harmful_failure_paths: One payment flag advances while its coupled flag remains on the old behavior.
A global flag dependency turns a control-plane outage into a four-region service outage.
incomplete_information: The maximum business-safe duration for each noncritical flag class has not been agreed.
public.reversible_action: Canary the classified contract on one non-payment service and replay regional isolation plus rollback.

must_cover_constraints: Separate ordinary divergent flags from coupled safety-critical flags.
Provide a locally observable pinned or stale state during isolation.
Keep the flag control plane out of ordinary request availability.
Use an epoch or equivalent barrier for coupled changes.
Make rollback preserve the same consistency boundary as rollout.
acceptable_decision_families: Classified flags with local pinned fallbacks, atomic epochs for coupled flags, and an isolation canary.
A narrow strongly coordinated safety class alongside locally available eventual classes.
fatal_errors: Making every service request synchronously depend on the home flag region.
Permitting coupled payment controls to advance or roll back independently.
known_valid_insights: Consistency should follow the coupling risk of the flag rather than one global policy.
decision_closure_requirements: Name the first service and flag class.
Define isolation behavior for every class.
Set divergence and stale-state stop thresholds.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward a control-plane design that reserves coordination for true coupling and preserves local service availability.

public.objective: Rotate a suspected storage encryption key without losing the ability to restore long-lived archives or extending normal trust indefinitely.
facts: Production objects use envelope encryption and can be rewrapped without rewriting payloads.
Monthly archives are immutable for seven years and store encrypted data keys beside each object.
Restore workers fetch key metadata only when a restore starts.
The current master key is suspected of exposure but no misuse has been confirmed.
A full archive rewrap would take about eleven months at the available throughput.
public.decision_request: Choose the rotation sequence, archive restore path, emergency revocation trigger, and completion evidence.
plausible_paths: Disable the old key immediately and accept failed archive restores.
Rotate production now and use a bounded isolated archive decrypt-and-rewrap service.
Keep the old key generally enabled until all seven years of archives are rewrapped.
material_constraints: Archived data must remain restorable for retention obligations.
The suspected key may need emergency revocation before rewrap completes.
Immutable archives cannot have payloads rewritten.
Restore workers currently assume ordinary key availability.
Completion cannot be inferred only from production-object rewrap counts.
harmful_failure_paths: An emergency key disablement makes required archives permanently unreadable.
The suspected key remains broadly usable for years while background rewrap proceeds.
incomplete_information: The number of archive restores that can tolerate a manual approval delay is unknown.
public.reversible_action: Test an isolated approved restore flow against copied archive metadata while leaving the production restore path unchanged.

must_cover_constraints: Separate production rotation from long-tail archive recovery.
Narrow old-key use to an isolated auditable restore boundary.
Define a viable response to confirmed misuse.
Validate restore behavior before disabling ordinary key access.
Measure archive recoverability rather than only rewrap progress.
acceptable_decision_families: Immediate production rotation plus an isolated time-bounded legacy decrypt service and tested emergency recovery.
Cohort rewrap with approved archive restores, usage monitoring, and a confirmed-misuse kill path.
fatal_errors: Disabling the only archive decryption path before recovery tests pass.
Leaving the suspected key available to ordinary workloads until the entire archive ages out.
known_valid_insights: A narrow restore-only boundary can preserve recoverability without preserving general trust.
decision_closure_requirements: Name the first production and archive cohorts.
Set old-key access scope and owner.
Define rollback and final retirement conditions.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward independent treatment of normal production trust and exceptional archive recoverability.

cases.title: AI meeting summaries with uneven consent

public.objective: Test AI meeting summaries without surprising non-customer participants or making deletion promises the system cannot honor.
facts: Enterprise hosts can enable recording, but external guests may join by phone without an account.
The summary model receives diarized transcript segments after the meeting ends.
Audio is deleted after seven days, while transcripts currently remain for ninety days.
Workspace administrators can export summaries but cannot yet export the underlying consent event.
The pilot customer wants automatic summaries for every sales call next month.
public.decision_request: Choose the pilot cohort, notice and consent behavior, retention boundary, and expansion evidence.
plausible_paths: Enable every sales call automatically under the host's workspace setting.
Run an explicit-notice opt-in pilot with bounded retention and auditable consent.
Delay all testing until every guest has a product account.
material_constraints: Phone guests may not see in-product notice.
Transcript retention outlives audio retention.
Administrators need evidence of the consent state governing a summary.
A host preference does not communicate directly with every participant.
Deletion must cover transcript, summary, exports, and queued model work.
harmful_failure_paths: A phone guest is summarized without receiving an effective notice or choice.
The product reports deletion while queued processing or exported summary data remains accessible.
incomplete_information: The pilot customer's jurisdictions and required consent standard have not been classified.
public.reversible_action: Run internal meetings with simulated phone guests and verify notice, refusal, deletion, and audit events end to end.

must_cover_constraints: Provide an out-of-band notice or consent path for phone guests.
Avoid claiming a universal legal basis while classification is unresolved.
Align transcript and summary retention for the pilot.
Include queued work and exports in deletion evidence.
Make consent state exportable and auditable.
acceptable_decision_families: A jurisdiction-bounded explicit opt-in pilot with phone notice, short retention, and deletion proof.
Internal validation followed by a narrow customer cohort only after consent classification and audit export work.
fatal_errors: Treating the host setting as sufficient proof that every phone participant agreed.
Claiming complete erasure while model jobs or governed summary copies can still materialize.
known_valid_insights: Participant reachability and lifecycle evidence are separate launch gates.
decision_closure_requirements: Specify the phone-participant notice and refusal path.
Set pilot retention and deletion timing.
Define stop, rollback, and expansion criteria.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward a useful narrow pilot that does not invent legal certainty or ignore non-account participants.

cases.title: API deprecation with offline signed requests

public.objective: Retire an insecure API version without rejecting legitimate offline-signed requests or accepting replayed legacy operations indefinitely.
facts: The old API signs requests without including the endpoint version in the signature.
Some field devices queue signed requests for up to fourteen days before reconnecting.
The new API includes a version and nonce but requires a firmware update.
Thirty-one percent of active devices have not checked in during the last week.
The gateway can translate payload shape but cannot safely manufacture a new device signature.
public.decision_request: Choose the cutoff sequence, legacy queue treatment, replay protection, and completion evidence.
plausible_paths: Reject every old-version request on the announced cutoff date.
Use a bounded drain window for pre-cutoff signed requests with an independent replay ledger.
Continue accepting old signatures until every device upgrades.
material_constraints: Legitimate queued requests can arrive after the cutoff.
The old signature does not bind the request to one API version.
Payload translation does not upgrade authentication strength.
Long-offline devices need a recovery path.
The system must distinguish pre-cutoff work from newly replayed legacy requests.
harmful_failure_paths: A replayed old signature remains valid throughout an unbounded migration.
The cutoff strands offline devices with no authenticated upgrade or recovery route.
incomplete_information: The distribution of business-critical operations in long-delayed queues is unknown.
public.reversible_action: Shadow-classify old signed requests by signing time, device, and nonce surrogate before enforcing any rejection.

must_cover_constraints: Separate queued pre-cutoff operations from post-cutoff legacy signing.
Use durable replay detection across gateway retries.
Avoid presenting payload translation as signature migration.
Provide a recovery path for long-offline devices.
Define retirement evidence beyond recent check-in rate.
acceptable_decision_families: A bounded authenticated drain with replay ledger, firmware cohorts, and offline recovery.
Shadow classification followed by staged cutoff of new legacy signatures while honoring verified queued work.
fatal_errors: Accepting all old signatures until every device eventually reconnects.
Rejecting delayed devices without an authenticated path to obtain the new firmware or credential.
known_valid_insights: A drain window is safe only if it distinguishes previously authorized work from fresh replay.
decision_closure_requirements: Set offline recovery and support behavior.
Define stop, rollback, and final cutoff evidence.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward staged authentication retirement that closes replay risk without assuming all work is online.

public.objective: Resume a paused account-store migration without losing writes or treating a partial backfill rollback as data recovery.
facts: The new store has received dual writes for six days and contains ninety-four percent of accounts.
A retry bug created duplicate address records for about 0.4 percent of migrated accounts.
The old store remains the read source, but one background worker reads from the new store.
Dual-write failures are logged asynchronously and may be delayed by twenty minutes.
The backfill cursor can restart, but it does not record the source version read for each account.
public.decision_request: Choose containment, repair, resume criteria, and rollback evidence.
plausible_paths: Delete the new store and restart the migration from zero.
Freeze affected writers, reconcile by version, repair duplicates, and resume a bounded cohort.
Continue backfill because the old store remains the primary read path.
material_constraints: One production worker already consumes new-store state.
Delayed dual-write logs do not prove current parity.
A cursor restart can overwrite newer destination state with older source data.
Duplicate repair must preserve legitimate repeated addresses.
Rollback must account for writes accepted only by one store.
harmful_failure_paths: A restarted backfill silently replaces newer destination changes with stale source versions.
Operators declare rollback while the background worker continues acting on corrupted new-store records.
incomplete_information: The number of dual writes accepted only by the new store is not yet known.
public.reversible_action: Pause the background consumer and replay a small version-aware reconciliation sample without applying mutations.

must_cover_constraints: Contain every reader and writer that touches the new store.
Reconcile source and destination using version or equivalent causality.
Account for delayed and one-sided dual writes.
Repair duplicates using domain identity rather than row count.
Separate a logical rollback claim from deleting destination data.
acceptable_decision_families: Full consumer containment followed by version-aware reconciliation and a small resume canary.
Keep the destination isolated, build a write ledger, repair deterministically, then resume only after parity evidence.
fatal_errors: Restarting the cursor without protecting destination updates that are newer than the source snapshot.
Calling the incident rolled back while a production consumer still uses the affected destination.
known_valid_insights: When both stores can accept unique writes, deleting one side is not automatically rollback.
decision_closure_requirements: Name all contained readers and writers.
Define the reconciliation identity and version rule.
Set resume cohort and stop thresholds.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward causal reconciliation and full containment over simplistic restart or delete actions.

public.objective: Reduce paging noise from a flapping dependency without hiding customer-impacting failures or making the dependency team the sole detector.
facts: A third-party identity endpoint has flapped for twenty minutes on four mornings this week.
Twenty-seven services emit individual pages even though most requests retry successfully.
Three services cannot retry because they perform interactive device authorization.
The vendor status feed has reported incidents between eight and thirty minutes after internal detection.
The paging system can group alerts by dependency but has no current customer-impact signal.
public.decision_request: Choose suppression scope, fallback signals, ownership, and exit criteria.
plausible_paths: Mute every alert mentioning the identity vendor for one hour.
Group retry-tolerant alerts while preserving independent impact pages and synthetic checks.
Leave all pages unchanged until the vendor permanently fixes the endpoint.
material_constraints: Three services have direct user impact without retry protection.
Vendor status is delayed and cannot be the only trigger.
Grouping must not erase service-specific saturation or fallback failures.
Suppression state needs an owner and automatic expiry.
Customer-impact telemetry is not yet uniformly available.
harmful_failure_paths: A broad mute hides failure of the interactive authorization path.
Operators wait for the vendor feed while internal synthetic checks already show impact.
incomplete_information: The fraction of retried requests that later fail at the business-operation level is unknown.
public.reversible_action: Apply grouping only to two retry-tolerant services for one incident window while retaining raw events and synthetic alerts.

must_cover_constraints: Exclude non-retrying interactive services from broad suppression.
Retain independent internal detection rather than relying on vendor status.
Preserve visibility into fallback and saturation failures.
Give suppression an expiry and named owner.
Collect business-impact evidence before expansion.
acceptable_decision_families: A small grouping canary for retry-tolerant services with preserved synthetic and impact signals.
Dependency-level deduplication plus service escape hatches, expiry, and raw-event retention.
fatal_errors: Muting all vendor-related pages including services with no functional retry path.
Using the delayed vendor status page as the sole signal that an internal incident exists.
known_valid_insights: Noise reduction is safe only when an independent impact path survives aggregation.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward targeted aggregation with surviving independent detection and explicit expiry.

cases.title: Consent withdrawal for derived voice embeddings

public.objective: Honor voice-feature consent withdrawal across raw clips and derived embeddings without claiming that aggregate models can be selectively untrained today.
facts: Users may opt in to speaker recognition and later withdraw consent.
Raw clips are stored for thirty days, while per-user embeddings persist until account deletion.
A shared acoustic model was fine-tuned quarterly using a mixture of opted-in clips.
The serving cache can retain an embedding for up to six hours after the database row is deleted.
The current audit log records deletion requests but not cache eviction or training-set membership.
public.decision_request: Choose withdrawal semantics, cache and pipeline controls, user communication, and completion evidence.
plausible_paths: Promise immediate removal from every current and historical model.
Stop future use, delete user-specific artifacts and caches, and disclose bounded aggregate-model limits.
Keep all embeddings until account deletion because the user originally opted in.
material_constraints: User-specific embeddings and shared model parameters have different removal properties.
Serving caches can outlive the database delete.
Queued training jobs may already reference clips.
The audit trail cannot currently prove pipeline-wide completion.
Product wording must not invent selective unlearning capability.
harmful_failure_paths: Recognition continues from a cache after withdrawal is recorded complete.
The product promises selective historical untraining that the pipeline cannot perform.
incomplete_information: Policy has not defined whether the next shared-model refresh is sufficient treatment for prior aggregate contribution.
public.reversible_action: Test withdrawal on internal identities with delayed cache eviction and a queued training job.

must_cover_constraints: Separate deletion of user-specific artifacts from aggregate-model treatment.
Evict serving caches and fence queued jobs.
Avoid unsupported claims about historical untraining.
Create cross-system completion evidence.
Provide a truthful user-visible withdrawal outcome.
acceptable_decision_families: Immediate future-use stop with artifact and cache deletion, job fencing, and explicit aggregate-model limits.
A staged withdrawal contract with auditable serving shutdown now and governed shared-model refresh treatment.
fatal_errors: Reporting withdrawal complete while the serving cache can still identify the user.
Guaranteeing removal from every historical model without a mechanism capable of providing it.
known_valid_insights: A truthful withdrawal contract distinguishes identifiable artifacts from diffuse aggregate influence.
decision_closure_requirements: State the aggregate-model limitation to users.
Set rollout, stop, and correction criteria.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward concrete future-use prevention and honest limits rather than impossible erasure claims.

cases.title: Cross-tenant telemetry during rare incident diagnosis

public.objective: Diagnose a rare multi-tenant failure without turning emergency telemetry into unbounded cross-tenant data access.
facts: The failure occurs about once per ten million requests and has not reproduced in staging.
Existing logs contain tenant identifiers but omit request payload fragments needed to distinguish two parser paths.
Engineers propose a seven-day one-percent payload capture across all tenants.
The logging platform supports tenant-specific encryption keys but not field-level retention policies.
Two regulated tenants have contracts that prohibit payload logging.
public.decision_request: Choose capture scope, access controls, retention, and evidence for ending or expanding the diagnostic.
plausible_paths: Capture one percent of payloads from every tenant for seven days.
Use eligible-tenant sampling with parser-path fingerprints and break-glass access.
Wait for the defect to reproduce in staging before collecting any new evidence.
material_constraints: Two tenants cannot be included in payload capture.
The event is too rare for a tiny undirected sample to guarantee evidence.
The logging platform lacks field-specific deletion.
Cross-tenant access requires auditable purpose limitation.
The diagnostic must have an automatic end condition.
harmful_failure_paths: Contract-prohibited tenant payloads enter the diagnostic store.
A temporary capture remains enabled after the incident question is answered.
incomplete_information: It is unknown whether parser fingerprints alone can distinguish the failing path.
public.reversible_action: Replay synthetic payloads to validate fingerprints, then canary encrypted capture on one eligible internal tenant.

must_cover_constraints: Exclude prohibited tenants before data leaves the request path.
Test lower-content fingerprints before broad payload collection.
Use purpose-bound access with audit and tenant-aware encryption.
Compensate for the platform's coarse retention capability.
Set automatic expiry and evidence-based expansion.
acceptable_decision_families: Fingerprint-first diagnosis followed by a small eligible-tenant encrypted capture with break-glass access.
A contract-filtered canary using isolated storage, whole-object expiry, and a specific diagnostic stop condition.
fatal_errors: Sampling payloads before enforcing the regulated-tenant exclusion at collection time.
Enabling emergency cross-tenant capture without an expiry or named evidence question.
known_valid_insights: When deletion granularity is coarse, isolation and whole-object expiry become primary controls.
decision_closure_requirements: Set access owner and approval path.
Define diagnostic success, stop, and rollback.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward minimum necessary evidence with enforceable exclusions and an expiring diagnostic contract.

cases.title: Managed database migration with weak exit tooling

public.objective: Reduce database operations burden without committing critical workloads to a provider before exit and recovery paths are proven.
facts: The managed service would reduce projected operations staffing by three engineers.
It supports continuous export, but exports omit provider-specific access policies and scheduled jobs.
The proposed three-year discount requires moving sixty percent of spend within nine months.
One critical workload uses an extension available only in the provider's premium tier.
The current self-hosted fleet has passed two regional recovery exercises this year.
public.decision_request: Choose commitment scope, migration sequence, exit proof, and negotiation conditions.
plausible_paths: Sign the full discount and migrate the critical workload first.
Negotiate a smaller commitment and migrate portable workloads after an exit rehearsal.
Keep every workload self-hosted for the entire three-year period.
material_constraints: Data export alone does not recreate policies or scheduled jobs.
The discount threshold creates schedule pressure before proof.
The premium extension increases both cost and technical dependence.
Recovery evidence for the managed service is not yet available.
A failed exit could outlast the staffing savings.
harmful_failure_paths: The company commits most spend before it can reconstruct exported workloads elsewhere.
The first migration uses the least portable workload and makes the pilot hard to reverse.
incomplete_information: The provider has not agreed to assistance or timing guarantees for a bulk exit.
public.reversible_action: Migrate one portable noncritical workload and restore its export into an independent environment.

must_cover_constraints: Treat policies and scheduled jobs as part of portability.
Avoid using the provider-specific extension as the first proof workload.
Tie commitment growth to tested recovery and exit evidence.
Include contractual exit assistance in negotiation.
Compare staffing savings with concentration and recovery cost.
acceptable_decision_families: A smaller staged commitment with portable workload canaries and independent restores.
A time-bounded managed-service pilot whose expansion depends on full-stack exit rehearsal and contract terms.
fatal_errors: Signing the majority-spend commitment before any independent restoration of a migrated workload.
Using the provider-only extension workload as the supposedly reversible first migration.
known_valid_insights: Exit proof must recreate operational policy, not just copy table data.
decision_closure_requirements: Set expansion, stop, and rollback conditions.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward staged economic commitment that follows technical exit evidence.

public.objective: Protect a product launch from component shortages without prebuying inventory that cannot be used if certification slips.
facts: The sole qualified radio module has a twenty-six-week lead time.
The supplier offers allocation only with a noncancelable order for twelve months of forecast demand.
The product has not completed regional radio certification.
A second module could be qualified in sixteen weeks but requires a board revision.
Forecast demand ranges from forty thousand to one hundred ten thousand units.
public.decision_request: Choose purchase volume, second-source work, certification gates, and downside controls.
plausible_paths: Prebuy the high forecast immediately to guarantee launch supply.
Secure a staged allocation with option-like tranches while starting second-source qualification.
Wait for certification before ordering any module.
material_constraints: The proposed order is noncancelable.
Certification delay can make early inventory unusable for the launch window.
The second source requires engineering capacity and a board change.
Demand uncertainty is wider than the supplier's commitment flexibility.
Launch supply and long-term supplier concentration are different risks.
harmful_failure_paths: The company owns a year of unusable modules after certification or schedule changes.
A minimal first order preserves cash but leaves no credible path through the supplier lead time.
incomplete_information: The supplier has not stated whether deposits can reserve later production slots without fixing final volume.
public.reversible_action: Negotiate and price a small certification batch plus later tranches before signing the annual order.

must_cover_constraints: Separate certification batch needs from full demand commitment.
Price downside across the forecast range.
Start or explicitly reject second-source work with an owner.
Seek commercial flexibility before treating the offered order as fixed.
Tie later volume to certification and demand evidence.
acceptable_decision_families: A small noncancelable tranche plus conditional allocation and immediate second-source qualification.
A negotiated option or deposit structure with certification gates and capped exposure.
fatal_errors: Committing to the high annual forecast before certification and without a reuse or resale path.
Waiting for final certification without reserving any capacity or developing an alternate source.
known_valid_insights: The valuable negotiation target is production-slot access without premature volume certainty.
decision_closure_requirements: Name the second-source owner and deadline.
Define negotiation fallback and decision date.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward real-option thinking that buys schedule protection while bounding irreversible inventory.

cases.title: Automated code fixes in a shared repository

public.objective: Use automated code fixes to reduce maintenance toil without allowing a central bot to bypass service ownership or silently widen production risk.
facts: A bot can generate dependency and lint fixes for eighty repositories.
Twenty repositories have reliable integration tests, while twelve have no active owner.
The bot currently opens pull requests but does not merge them.
Security wants critical patch latency below forty-eight hours.
Some repositories deploy automatically when a change reaches the default branch.
public.decision_request: Choose automation tiers, approval ownership, orphan-repository handling, and expansion evidence.
plausible_paths: Enable automatic merge for every bot pull request that passes available tests.
Use risk tiers with bounded auto-merge only for well-owned, well-tested low-risk changes.
Keep the bot advisory-only in every repository.
material_constraints: Passing available tests means little in repositories with weak coverage.
Default-branch merge can be a production deployment.
Orphan repositories lack an accountable approver.
Critical security latency needs an escalation path.
Dependency updates can change runtime behavior despite small diffs.
harmful_failure_paths: The bot merges into an auto-deploying orphan repository based on an empty or weak test suite.
A blanket manual policy leaves critical fixes waiting without an accountable escalation owner.
incomplete_information: The fraction of generated dependency fixes that require runtime configuration changes is unknown.
public.reversible_action: Canary auto-merge on owned libraries with strong tests and no automatic production deployment.

must_cover_constraints: Distinguish patch generation from merge and deployment authority.
Require ownership and meaningful test evidence for any auto-merge tier.
Treat orphan repositories as a governance defect, not a low-friction target.
Provide a bounded critical-security escalation path.
Separate low-risk lint fixes from behavior-changing dependencies.
acceptable_decision_families: Risk-tiered automation with a narrow library canary and human control over production-impacting changes.
Fast bot generation everywhere, bounded auto-merge only in owned nondeploying repositories, and explicit security escalation.
fatal_errors: Auto-merging into unowned repositories merely because all existing checks are green.
Treating every dependency update as equivalent to a behavior-free formatting change.
known_valid_insights: Faster patch generation does not require granting the same system universal merge authority.
decision_closure_requirements: Define the first eligible repository cohort.
Define rollback, stop, and expansion evidence.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward useful low-risk automation while preserving ownership at high-impact boundaries.

cases.title: Shared response-time metric across sales and support

public.objective: Improve enterprise response speed without rewarding superficial acknowledgements or causing sales and support to hide transfers.
facts: Enterprise customers complain that ownership is unclear during urgent issues.
Leadership proposes a shared metric of first response within fifteen minutes.
Sales can acknowledge quickly but usually cannot diagnose technical issues.
Support currently measures time to qualified triage and time to resolution.
Transfers between teams are not represented in the customer success dashboard.
public.decision_request: Choose the shared metric, ownership handoff, pilot cohort, and evidence for incentive expansion.
plausible_paths: Pay both teams on fifteen-minute first response for every enterprise issue.
Pilot a paired acknowledgement and qualified-ownership metric with visible transfers.
Keep separate team metrics and make no shared customer measure.
material_constraints: A fast acknowledgement may not create accountable technical ownership.
Sales should not be rewarded for delaying transfer after replying.
Support cannot control when sales forwards an issue.
Transfer visibility is missing from current reporting.
Incentives can change classification and channel behavior.
harmful_failure_paths: Teams optimize acknowledgements while customers still wait without a qualified owner.
Transfers or issue reclassification disappear from measurement and make the target look healthy.
incomplete_information: The baseline relationship between acknowledgement speed and customer outcome is unknown.
public.reversible_action: Run the paired metric as a noncompensated dashboard for one enterprise segment and audit transfers.

must_cover_constraints: Separate acknowledgement from qualified ownership.
Make handoff time and transfer count visible.
Avoid compensation before gaming and outcome relationships are measured.
Assign responsibility for delayed forwarding.
Include customer outcome or resolution evidence.
acceptable_decision_families: A shadow dashboard combining acknowledgement, qualified ownership, transfer quality, and customer outcome.
A narrow noncompensated pilot with explicit handoff clocks before any shared incentive.
fatal_errors: Paying on acknowledgement speed alone while ignoring whether a qualified owner accepts the issue.
Launching a shared incentive while cross-team transfers remain invisible to the metric.
known_valid_insights: The metric should reward transfer into accountable ownership, not merely the first message.
decision_closure_requirements: Define acknowledgement and qualified ownership separately.
Define stop, revision, and compensation-entry criteria.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward a shadow incentive pilot that measures the full handoff rather than a cosmetically fast first touch.
